_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Algorithm Problems: Part 2
summary: Track related state and handle empty inputs.
refresher: Multi-step algorithms may preserve a best value plus its index or combine a total with a count. Write an invariant and decide tie and empty-input rules.

blocks.body: Multi-step algorithms may preserve a best value plus its index or combine a total with a count. Write an invariant and decide tie and empty-input rules. Track related state and handle empty inputs.

A more involved algorithm often carries related state, such as a best index and the value available at that index. State an invariant in ordinary language: after examining positions through i, best names the largest one seen. That sentence tells you what each update must preserve.

blocks.caption: The empty guard precedes index access, and later values may replace the candidate index.
Handle empty input before creating an element-based candidate.

blocks.body: For `{4, 9, 7}`, best begins at zero, changes to one after comparing nine, and remains one after seven. Decide what ties mean by choosing `>` for the first maximum or `>=` for the last. The empty contract belongs in the function documentation.

blocks.title: Inspect this state
blocks.body: Without the empty check, reading element zero from an empty vector is invalid before the loop even begins.

blocks.why: The first element already supplies the current candidate, so only later positions need comparison.
An equal later value does not satisfy greater-than, preserving the earlier index.
